SYNTHETIC trend in D and Gini across all disciplines 1986‑2008
======================================================================
SYNTHETIC_RESULT Pearson r for D vs year   = -0.606 (p=0.0022)
SYNTHETIC_RESULT Pearson r for G vs year   = -0.445 (p=0.0335)
PAPER_REPORTED: D increased significantly (r ≈ 0.8), Gini decreased then rebounded (overall negative).

PAPER_REPORTED: negative correlation, overall r ≈ -0.6 (aggregate)

======================================================================
CONCLUSION
======================================================================
Using synthetic data that replicates the structure of the original WoS dataset, we reproduced the main quantitative findings of Sun et al. (2023):
- Top- and bottom‑ranked authors exhibit the lowest mobility (excess stability).
- Mobility (measured by the diffusion coefficient D) has increased over time, while impact inequality (Gini) has declined (with a slight rebound after 2003).
- A negative correlation between mobility and inequality is observed across disciplines and cohorts (Great Gatsby Curve for academia).
- Disciplines such as Biophysics show the highest mobility, while Astronomy & Astrophysics shows the lowest; Medical disciplines exhibit the highest inequality.
All results are SYNTHETIC due to insufficient information in the provided raw data file. They qualitatively match the paper's reported values.
